name: Howlin' Wolf
abstract: Chester Arthur Burnett (June 10, 1910 – January 10, 1976), better known by his stage name Howlin' Wolf, was an American blues singer, guitarist and harmonica player. He was at the forefront of transforming acoustic Delta blues into electric Chicago blues, and over a four-decade career, recorded blues, rhythm and blues, rock and roll, and…

about_quotes.speaker: Burrill Crohn
about_quotes.evidence: Don McGlynn who did Louie Prima and Harold Arlen and Mingus and Horace Parlan and Howlin’ Wolf.
about_quotes.doc_title: Burrill Crohn interviewed by Monk Rowe, Woodstock, New York, January 3, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Lloyd
about_quotes.evidence: That’s when I played with those guys, like Howlin’ Wolf... Howlin’ Wolf was the baddest cat that I ever played with on that circuit.
about_quotes.doc_title: Charles Lloyd — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chuck Mancuso
about_quotes.evidence: But Muddy Waters, you know, and Howlin’ Wolf, and these guys in the cities making this loud music as they plugged the things in, they were putting those people down.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chuck Mancuso interviewed by Monk Rowe, Buffalo, New York, October 10, 2013

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Griffin
about_quotes.evidence: Howlin’ Wolf would be there the next week... had some local guys that there was always music on the weekend.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Griffin interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 12, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Dorothy Donegan
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Tell me about, you mentioned Howlin’ Wolf, I think he might have come a little later.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dorothy Donegan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: He got Howlin’ Wolf about $500,000 in back royalties, so this is the kind of stuff he was doing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
